skill.name: managing-contacts
skill.description: Guides Mailtrap contact management with API endpoints, bulk imports, lists, custom fields, custom events, and CRM sync workflows.
skill.summary: Manage Mailtrap marketing contacts, imports, lists, fields, events, and audience sync workflows.
skill.author: mailtrap

security_audit.summary: Static findings for dynamic imports, shell backticks, path traversal, and weak cryptography are false positives from Markdown text, API path examples, and YAML syntax. The skill does include legitimate Mailtrap API URLs and curl examples that use bearer tokens and can change contact data. No prompt injection attempt, hidden execution path, data exfiltration pattern, or malicious intent was found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Documented Mailtrap API Calls Require Token Care
low_findings.description: The skill includes curl examples that send Authorization: Bearer $MAILTRAP_API_TOKEN to mailtrap.io and can create contacts, imports, and events. This is expected for a Mailtrap contact management skill, but users should protect tokens and review payloads before execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The curl examples and authorization header are explicit, and they target the expected Mailtrap API. Risk is low because the behavior matches the skill purpose and does not send secrets to an unrelated domain.

low_findings.title: Static Code Execution Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported dynamic import and backtick execution hits occur in Markdown table labels, inline code, or fenced curl examples. I found no script file, dynamic import expression, eval behavior, or hidden execution path in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows documentation text and Markdown code fences, not executable JavaScript, Ruby, or shell launched by the skill. The findings are explained by words such as import and by Markdown backtick formatting.

low_findings.title: Static Filesystem And Crypto Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography hit maps to YAML description syntax on line 3, and path traversal hits map to abbreviated API paths with ellipses on lines 46 and 93. No file read, file write, path join, or cryptographic operation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain front matter syntax and shortened API route notation only. There is no filesystem API use or cryptographic algorithm reference in the reviewed file.

content.value_statement: Marketing teams need accurate contact data before they launch campaigns or automations. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code plan Mailtrap contact API work with correct endpoints and safer token handling.
actual_capabilities: Identifies Mailtrap contact endpoints for create, update, delete, import, export, lists, fields, and events.
Explains required account ID and bearer token placement for Mailtrap Contacts API requests.
Provides curl examples for single contact creation, bulk import, and custom contact events.
Distinguishes marketing contacts, lists, segments, custom fields, custom events, and sending suppressions.
Recommends bulk imports and backoff when contact volume could hit rate limits.
Points users to Mailtrap documentation and OpenAPI references before building request bodies.
limitations: It does not include executable integration code or a packaged client library.
It cannot validate live Mailtrap credentials, account IDs, list IDs, or field IDs by itself.
It relies on current Mailtrap documentation for exact request body schemas.
It does not manage consent policy decisions or legal compliance for marketing contacts.

use_cases.title: Plan a CRM Contact Sync
use_cases.description: Map CRM fields to Mailtrap custom fields and choose create, update, or bulk import flows.

use_cases.title: Prepare Campaign Audiences
use_cases.description: Organize lists, segments, and custom fields before scheduling a Mailtrap marketing campaign.

use_cases.title: Debug Contact Automation Inputs
use_cases.description: Check custom event names, parameters, and contact identifiers used by Mailtrap automations.

prompt_templates.title: Choose the right contact action
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner user deciding where to start.
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to manage Mailtrap marketing contacts. Ask me for the goal, contact volume, and available IDs, then recommend the right endpoint group.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a single contact request
prompt_templates.scenario: Create or update one contact with attributes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me prepare a Mailtrap contact create request using email, list IDs, and custom fields. Include token and account ID placeholders only.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a bulk import
prompt_templates.scenario: Move many contacts from a CSV, CRM, or warehouse.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Mailtrap bulk contact import plan for my source data. Include batching, field mapping, rate limit handling, and import status polling.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an audience sync workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my proposed Mailtrap contact sync workflow for token handling, contact identity, list membership changes, custom fields, custom events, and rollback risk.

output_examples.input: I need to import 35,000 webinar leads into Mailtrap and assign them to two lists.
output: Use the bulk import endpoint because the volume is high.
Prepare one import job with email, field mappings, and included list IDs.
Poll the import status and record failed rows for correction.

output_examples.input: I want to trigger an automation when a known contact logs in.
output_examples.output: Use a custom contact event with the contact identifier, event name, and a small parameter set. Confirm the automation expects the same event name.

output_examples.input: My team is confused about suppressions and marketing segments.
output_examples.output: Treat suppressions as sending-side delivery blocks. Treat lists, segments, consent fields, and custom attributes as marketing audience controls.

best_practices: Check the Mailtrap OpenAPI spec before generating request bodies.
Use environment variables for API tokens and account IDs instead of hardcoded values.
Use bulk imports, batching, and backoff for large contact loads.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real API tokens into prompts, examples, or shared logs.
Do not create contacts one at a time when a bulk import fits the workload.
Do not treat marketing segments as a replacement for sending suppressions.

faq.question: Does this skill send requests to Mailtrap automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. The user or agent must choose whether to run any command.

faq.question: Which credentials are needed for the API examples?
faq.answer: The examples use a Mailtrap API token and a Mailtrap account ID. Store both as environment variables.

faq.question: Can it help with bulk imports?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains the bulk import endpoint, the 50,000 contact request limit, and status polling.

faq.question: Does it cover Mailtrap custom fields?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers custom fields as contact attributes and points to the related Mailtrap documentation.

faq.question: Does it manage unsubscribes or hard bounces?
faq.answer: It explains that sending suppressions are separate from marketing contacts and segments.

faq.question: Can it verify my live account configuration?
faq.answer: No. It cannot validate live list IDs, field IDs, account access, or token scopes without external API checks.
